coredata.dc:title: Photovoltaic performance improvement of organic solar cell with ZnO nanorod arrays as electron transport layer using carbon quantum dots-incorporated photoactive layer
coredata.dc:creator: Issa, Nour Attallah
Yap, Chi Chin
Tan, Sin Tee
Hong, Kai Jeat
Lau, Kam Sheng
Khairulaman, Farah Liyana
Chia, Chin Hua
Hj Jumali, Mohammad Hafizuddin
Chong, Kok-Keong
coredata.dc:description: The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with astonishing optical and electrical features have been utilized to improve the photovoltaic performance of organic solar cells (OSCs). In this work, CQDs dispersed in isopropanol were incorporated into photoactive layer of poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT):(6,6)-phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester (PC61BM) with different volume concentrations (0, 20, 40, 60 and 80 vt%). ZnO nanorod arrays, serving as the electron transport layer, was prepared on ZnO seed layer-coated fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass substrates. The CQDs-incorporated P3HT:PC61BM photoactive layers were then spin-coated on top of ZnO and silver (Ag), acting as the top electrode, was deposited via thermal evaporation method. It was found that 40 vt% CQDs-incorporated device demonstrated the highest power conversion efficiency and enhancement of 37% compared to that of the device without CQDs. It is proven that the introduction of CQDs improves the optical absorption, exciton dissociation and charge transport of the OSCs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Exciton dissociation
Organic solar cell
Photoactive layer
ZnO nanorod Arrays

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank Malaysian Ministry of Higher Education for providing financial support via FRGS/1/2020/STG07/UKM/02/4 research grant. The authors also acknowledge Centre for Research and Instrumentation Management (CRIM) Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia for the analytical services given.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a). J−V characteristics of OSCs with different concentrations of CQDs under AM 1.5G 100 mW/cm2 illumination, (b) Voc, (c) Jsc (d) FF and (e) PCE as a function of CQDs concentration.

(a) TEM image of synthesized CQDs, FESEM cross-section images for (b) pristine, (c) 20 vt%, (d) 40 vt%, (e) 60 vt% and (f) 80 vt% samples, (g) schematic illustration of photoactive material infiltration within the ZnO nanorods.
(a) The UV–vis absorption spectra and (b) normalized absorption spectra of photoactive layers with different concentrations of CQDs.
(a) Raman spectra of the photoactive layers and (b) I1446/I1378ratio with different concentrations of CQDs.
(a) The PL spectra of samples with different concentrations of CQDs and (b) carrier transport mechanism.
(a) IPCE spectra of devices with different concentrations of CQDs, and (b) UV-VIS absorption of CQDs.
Photovoltaic parameters of OSCs with different concentrations of CQDs.

head.ce:title: Photovoltaic performance improvement of organic solar cell with ZnO nanorod arrays as electron transport layer using carbon quantum dots-incorporated photoactive layer

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Lee Kong Chian Faculty of Engineering and Science, Universiti Tunku Abdul Rahman, Jalan Sungai Long, Bandar Sungai Long, 43000, Kajang, Selangor, Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with astonishing optical and electrical features have been utilized to improve the photovoltaic performance of organic solar cells (OSCs). In this work, CQDs dispersed in isopropanol were incorporated into photoactive layer of poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT):(6,6)-phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester (PC61BM) with different volume concentrations (0, 20, 40, 60 and 80 vt%). ZnO nanorod arrays, serving as the electron transport layer, was prepared on ZnO seed layer-coated fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass substrates. The CQDs-incorporated P3HT:PC61BM photoactive layers were then spin-coated on top of ZnO and silver (Ag), acting as the top electrode, was deposited via thermal evaporation method. It was found that 40 vt% CQDs-incorporated device demonstrated the highest power conversion efficiency and enhancement of 37% compared to that of the device without CQDs. It is proven that the introduction of CQDs improves the optical absorption, exciton dissociation and charge transport of the OSCs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: One of the most appropriate ways to address the expected energy issue of the world is to use the power of sun. Organic solar cell (OSC) based on organic molecule and conjugated polymer is a promising alternative to traditional inorganic solar cell due to its low-cost production, ease of processing, lightweight, mechanical flexibility, sufficiently high power conversion efficiency (PCE) particularly for indoor light application [1–5]. Nevertheless, the PCEs of OSCs reported so far are still much lower than that of traditional inorganic solar cells [6]. One of the widely studied photoactive blend layers is consisted of poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT) as a donor and (6,6)-phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester (PC61BM) as an acceptor [7]. Incorporating a third component in the photoactive blend layer to make ternary OSCs has been reported as one of the effective approaches to improve the photovoltaic performance of OSCs [4,8–13].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) show great properties such as small size, low toxicity, small tunable bandgap, excellent electrical and optical properties [14]. Ternary OSCs using CQDs as the third component in the photoactive blend layer have been reported to obtain better photovoltaic performance. For example, Liu et al. achieved a 28% improvement in the PCE by introducing CQDs into the photoactive layer of PCDTBT:PC71BM [15]. In 2016, Zhang et al. fabricated ternary OSCs based on the photoactive layer of P3HT:ICBA:carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) and achieved a 43% enhancement of the PCE compared to the device without CNPs [16]. More recently in 2018, significant PCE improvement in OSCs based on the photoactive layer of P3HT:PC61BM has also been reported by Cui et al. and Lim et al. [17,18]. In general, introducing CQDs in the photoactive blend layer could enhance light absorption, exciton dissociation, and charge transport.

Almost all the previous studies on the utilization of CQDs were conducted on OSCs with electron transport layers (polyethylenimine ethoxylated, amine-rich polymer, TiO2 and ZnO) in thin-film form. In this work, instead of the electron transport layer in thin-film form, the effects of incorporating CQDs on the photovoltaic performance of OSCs with ZnO electron transport layer in nanorod form, which has not been reported so far, were investigated. It is unknown whether the incorporation of CQDs could effectively enhance the photovoltaic performance of OSCs with an electron transport layer other than in thin-film form. The role played by CQDs in this type of OSCs might be different, which is worth further investigation. In addition, the way of incorporating CQDs into the photoactive layer solution was also different from the previous works. In this work, the CQDs were first dispersed in isopropanol before mixing with the polymer blend solution. It is believed that the CQDs could be dispersed better in isopropanol compared to chlorobenzene used to dissolve the P3HT and PC61BM [19,20]. Various concentrations of CQDs have been incorporated into the photoactive layer. The optimized device with 40 vt% CQDs displayed almost 37% increase in PCE with respect to that without CQDs. The significant photovoltaic performance improvement was due to the enhanced light absorption, exciton dissociation and charge transport. The result indicates that a similar photovoltaic performance improvement could be achieved when ZnO nanorod arrays were used as electron transport layer upon the incorporation of CQDs as a third component in the photoactive layer.

CQDs were prepared from a precursor solution that consisted of 5 mmol of sodium citrate (Merck, >99%) and 50 mmol of urea (Merck, ≥ 99.5%) in 10 mL deionised water (1.8 MΩ, Merck). The solution was first stirred for 10 min and then placed in a commercial microwave oven (Panasonic, NN-SM330 M) for 5 min. The solution was later heated until boiling, and then a dark precipitate was formed. Next, the CQDs suspension was cooled to room temperature before subjected to the solvent exchange process. Next, the precipitate was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 30 min to collect the precipitate aiming to remove the secondary growth impurities. The precipitate was further dried in a vacuum oven for 24 h. Finally, 28 mg of CQDs paste was dispersed in 15 mL of isopropanol (IPA) and stirred at 1000 rpm for 30 min.

To prepare ZnO seed layer, 0.2 M of zinc acetate dehydrate (Sigma Aldrich, >99%) was first dissolved in a solution consisting of 6 mL of ethanol (Sigma Aldrich, > 99%) and 153 μL of diethanolamine (DEA; Sigma-Aldrich, >99%). The solution was stirred at 500 rpm with a heating temperature of 60 °C for 30 min. The solution was subsequently left for ageing for 24 h. Later, the FTO-coated glass substrates (Dyesol, ∼8Ω/Sq, 1.5 cm × 2.0 cm) were cleaned with acetone and isopropanol. Then, 100 μl of ZnO sol-gel solution was spin-coated on the FTO substrate at 3000 rpm for 30 s. This process was repeated three times. The sample was subsequently heated at 120 °C for 10 min. Finally, the sample was annealed in a muffle furnace (WiseTherm, FP-03) at 300 °C for 30 min.

To grow ZnO nanorod arrays, 0.04 M equimolar zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Alfa Aesar, 99.0%) and hexamethylenediamine, HMT (Sigma-Aldrich, ≥ 99.5%) were first dissolved in 20 mL of deionised water. The ZnO seed layer-coated FTO substrate was then immersed and laid at a 45° tilting angle against the sample vial to avoid the sediment of the secondary growth product. The ZnO nanorod preparation process was carried out at 90 °C in a heating oven for 20 min. Later, the sample was cleaned using a copious of deionised water to remove the sediment and dried at 100 °C for 10 min.

The ZnO nanorod array sample was used as an electron transporting layer in the inverted type OSC. The photoactive layer used in this work was a blend of P3HT donor (Rieke Metals, 4002-E) and PC61BM acceptor (LUMTEC, LT-S905, ≥99.5%). The photoactive layer was prepared with a weight ratio of 1:1 in chlorobenzene solution at a concentration of 36 mg/mL. The solution was stirred at 850 rpm at 60 °C. After 2 h, 18 μL of 1,8-octanedithiol, ODT (Sigma Aldrich, ≥ 97.0%) was added to the solution. Later, the mixture was continuously stirred for 22 h. In this work, different volume percentages (x = 0, 20, 40, 60 and 80 vt%) of CQDs were added to the as-prepared photoactive layer solution. The corresponding weight ratios of CQDs and P3HT: PC61BM are 0, 1.04, 2.08, 3.12 and 4.16 wt%.

For device fabrication, photoactive layer solutions with different x - vt% of CQDs were spin-coated onto the ZnO nanorod arrays at 1150 rpm for 60 s. The samples were post-baked at 120 °C for 10 min. The photoactive layer deposition was conducted in a glove box with humidity of ∼15% and a temperature of ∼25 °C. A 100 nm silver (Ag) electrode was deposited on the photoactive layer using a thermal evaporator operated at a vacuum condition of 5 × 10−5 mbar. The active area of the devices was 0.07 cm2.

For sample characterization, the cross-section images were obtained using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Zeiss Merlin Compact) at an accelerating voltage of 3 kV. The optical absorption spectra of samples were determined using a UV–Vis–NIR spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, Lambda 900) with scanning wavelength from 300 to 800 nm. Steady-state photoluminescence (PL) characteristics of samples were obtained using a fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburg, FLS920) at an excitation wavelength of 495 nm. In order to study the crystallinity, a Raman micro-spectroscopy system (Technospex, Raman-Ci) equipped with a 532 nm laser and an optical microscope (Nikon Eclipse Ci L) was used. The photovoltaic parameters of the devices, such as short circuit current density (Jsc), open-circuit voltage (Voc), fill factor (FF), and power conversion efficiency (PCE), were determined from current-voltage (I–V) measurements using Keithley 2401 source measurement unit under the illumination of a solar simulator at 100 mW/cm2 (Newport 96000, 150 W). Lastly, the incident photon to current conversion efficiency (IPCE) of the devices was recorded using an IPCE measurement system (Newport) at a chopping frequency of 10 Hz.

The current density-voltage (J−V) curves of the devices with different concentrations of CQDs are shown in Fig. 1 (a). The Jsc, Voc, FF and PCE of the devices are summarized in Table 1 and the results are typically average of five devices. The device without CQDs displayed a PCE of 1.74%, Voc of 0.48 V, Jsc of 7.90 mA/cm2, and FF of 49%, which is consistent with the PCE of the reported P3HT:PC61BM-based inverted OSCs [21–23]. In order to confirm that the improvement of PCE is mainly contributed by CQDs, pure propanol solvent (in the absence of CQDs) was added to the P3HT:PC61BM solution to fabricate a device. It was found that the PCE was slightly lower (1.58%) when propanol solvent was added. Therefore, it is clearly shown that the improvement in PCE was unlikely due to the propanol solvent.

The PCE of the devices improved first and then dropped when the concentration of CQDs surpassed 40 vt% as shown in Fig. 1(e). The device with 40 vt% CQDs exhibited the highest PCE of 2.39% among all the devices with Voc of 0.49 V, Jsc of 11.33 mA/cm2, and FF of 48%. The Jsc increased first and then decreased with increasing CQDs concentration. The maximum value of Jsc was obtained from the device with 40 vt% CQDs, as shown in Fig. 1(c). Unlike the Jsc, it was found that the Voc of devices only changed slightly as shown in Fig. 1(b), whereas the FF decreased gradually with increasing CQDs concentration, as shown in Fig. 1(d). Thus, the variations of PCE of the devices are mainly attributed to the change of their Jsc.

Several characterizations on the photoactive layer were carried out to understand the variation of device performance due to the CQDs incorporation. The TEM image of synthesized CQDs is given in Fig. 2 (a). The CQDs have typical sizes ranging from 2.0 to 5.5 nm with an average diameter of 3.8 ± 0.8 nm. The FESEM cross-sectional images for the samples are presented in Fig. 2(b–f). In this work, the growth time of the ZnO nanorod arrays was fixed at 20 min. The FESEM images reveal that the thicknesses of the ZnO nanorod arrays for all samples were measured to be 75 ± 4 nm. For the sample without CQDs, the thickness of the photoactive layer on top of the ZnO nanorod arrays layer was found to be 237 ± 18 nm. The thickness increased slightly to 276 ± 30 and 255 ± 21 nm, respectively when 20 and 40 vt% CQDs were incorporated into the photoactive layer. The small increase in thickness could be attributed to the additional CQDs in the photoactive layer. It is interesting to find that the thicknesses of the photoactive layer on top of the ZnO nanorod arrays layer increased significantly to 353 ± 29 and 363 ± 30 nm for 60 and 80 vt% CQDs samples, respectively. It is believed that the high-density aggregation and the agglomeration of CQDs at high concentration could limit the infiltration of the photoactive layer in between the ZnO nanorods, as illustrated in Fig. 2 (g), resulting in a thicker photoactive layer on top of the ZnO nanorod arrays. The poor infiltration also limits the electron transport from PC61BM or CQDs to ZnO, which is reflected in the decrement of FF and Jsc [24,25]. Different from the previous works on the OSC with electron transport layer in thin-film form, it is interesting to find out that the concentration of CQDs also affected the infiltration of the photoactive layer in between the ZnO nanorods in this work.

Fig. 3 (a) shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the samples with different concentrations of CQDs. The photoactive layer with 40 vt% CQDs shows a significant absorption improvement in the region of 350–650 nm compared to the sample without CQDs. As reported previously by other researchers, the CQDs could act as scattering centres to increase the light path length in the photoactive layer so that more photons can be absorbed, which makes a contribution to the increase of Jsc [18]. However, the absorption started to decrease when the concentration of CQDs was further increased to 60 and 80 vt%. It is believed that the effectiveness of CQDs as scattering centres reduces when agglomeration of CQDs appears. The normalized UV–vis absorption spectra of samples (Fig. 3(b)) show with the introduction of CQDs, a shoulder at ∼610 nm (P3) becomes more pronounced with the incorporation of CQDs, indicating enhanced ordering of P3HT and subsequently higher hole mobility in the P3HT region [26]. UV–Vis absorption characterization implies that not only the light absorption was improved, but the hole transport was also enhanced due to the introduction of CQDs in the photoactive layer. The improved hole transfer across the whole photoactive layer also contributes to higher Jsc.

Fig. 4 shows the Raman spectra of the samples with different concentrations of CQDs. It has been observed that the peak intensity ratio of 1446 cm−1 and 1378 cm−1 for sample without CQDs is the highest (5.44), in comparison to the corresponding ratios for samples with CQDs (4.13–4.36), indicating better ordering and hence crystallinity of P3HT [27–29]. This finding agrees well with the results obtained from normalized UV–Vis absorption spectra in Fig. 3(b). Although the ordering and crystallinity of P3HT increased with the concentration of CQDs, the Jsc was found to decrease when the concentration of CQDs exceeded 40 vt%. This result suggests that other factors contribute to the reduction of Jsc.

Fig. 5 (a) demonstrates the PL spectra of samples with different concentrations of CQDs. The PL intensity of P3HT: PC61BM film decreased significantly when the CQDs concentration increased from 0 to 40 vt% This result confirms more efficient exciton dissociation can be achieved in the photoactive layer, which results in higher Jsc.. As shown in Fig. 5(b), the electron energy level of the CQDs matches well with the electron energy level of the ZnO, PC61BM, and P3HT [18]. The band structure of CQDs narrows the energy barrier between the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) between P3HT and PCBM, which facilitates the dissociation of excitons and the transportation of electrons from P3HT towards the PCBM and ZnO. However, when the CQDs concentration was further increased to 60 and 80 vt%, the PL intensity increased. The PL intensity of 80 vt% CQDs sample was even higher than that of the sample without CQDs. In this case, many agglomerations could be created in the photoactive layer due to the addition of excess CQDs. The agglomerations would hinder P3HT and PC61BM from mixing evenly, thereby greatly reducing the excitons dissociation efficiency [30]. This factor also explains why a lower Jsc. was recorded for devices with high CQDs concentration, although the crystallinity of P3HT improved.

Fig. 6 (a) shows the IPCE spectra of devices with different concentrations of CQDs. The IPCE spectra follow a similar trend with the Jsc of the devices. The IPCE peak intensity initially increased as the CQDs concentration increased from 0 to 40 vt%. Nevertheless, the IPCE intensity decreased when CQDs concentration increased to 60 and 80 vt%. The CQDs have a strong optical absorption in the ultraviolet region and an absorption tail extending in to visible light region as shown in Fig. 6 (b). It is worth mentioning that there is no significant improvement in the region of 300–380 nm, where the prominent CQDs absorption peaks are located in, indicating the possible contribution of the down-conversion effect as reported by others could be eliminated in this case [31,32].

It is well known that the Voc of the OSCs is proportional to the difference between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of the donor and the LUMO of the acceptor [33]. At a moderate concentration of CQDs, it is believed that the CQDs also acted as an acceptor. As a result, the higher LUMO level of CQDs as shown in Fig. 5(b) enhanced the Voc to 0.49 V when the concentration of CQDs reached 40 vt%. However, at the highest concentration of CQDs (80 vt%), agglomeration of CQDs causes the charge recombination in the photoactive layer, which subsequently reduced Voc from 0.49 to 0.46 V. On the other hand, the FF shows a decreasing trend when the concentration of CQDs increases. The agglomeration of CQDs could hinder the transportation of electrons towards ZnO nanorods, thus increasing the series resistance and FF of the devices [16]. This observation is consistent with the FESEM analysis as shown in Fig. 2.

In summary, the effects of incorporating CQDs with different concentrations (0, 20, 40, 60 and 80 vt%) into the photoactive layer on the photovoltaic performance of OSCs with FTO/ZnO/P3HT:PC61BM:CQDs/Ag structure have been studied. The introduction of a moderate amount of CQDs into the photoactive layer increased the optical absorption, exciton dissociation efficiency and charge transport, leading to improvement in Jsc. However, the excess addition of CQDs resulted in agglomeration of CQDs in the photoactive layer, which limits the infiltration of photoactive materials in between ZnO nanorods and prevents the well mixing of P3HT and PC61BM. Consequently, all the photovoltaic parameters, including Jsc, Voc, FF and PCE, dropped significantly when the concentration of CQDs was increased to 80 vt%. The highest PCE of 2.39% was obtained at a CQDs concentration of 40 vt%. This finding also suggests that CQDs incorporating approach to improve photovoltaic performance can be extended to OSCs with an electron transport layer in nanorod form.

Nour Attallah Issa: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Chi Chin Yap: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition. Sin Tee Tan: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Kai Jeat Hong: Investigation. Kam Sheng Lau: Investigation. Farah Liyana Khairulaman: Investigation. Chin Hua Chia: Resources. Mohammad Hafizuddin Hj Jumali: Supervision, Resources. Kok-Keong Chong: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Malaysian Ministry of Higher Education for providing financial support via FRGS/1/2020/STG07/UKM/02/4 research grant. The authors also acknowledge Centre for Research and Instrumentation Management (CRIM) Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia for the analytical services given.
